serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of boiled pumpkin is approximately 100 g, equivalent to a small bowl or side portion as commonly served in Singaporean home meals.

nutrient_source.title: Boiled Pumpkin — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for boiled pumpkin (F14070002). Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, potassium, cholesterol, vitamin C, vitamin A, B-vitamins, vitamin D, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water are not reported in the HPB entry and are set to -1. USDA FoodData Central (NDB 11423, Pumpkin, cooked, boiled, drained, without salt) was consulted as a secondary cross-reference for plausibility validation.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — Pumpkin
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 75 for boiled pumpkin is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database and corroborated by Atkinson FS et al. (2008) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (75 × 4.9) / 100 = 3.7 per 100 g serving. Despite a high GI, the glycemic load per typical 100 g serving is low (3.7), making it clinically acceptable for most individuals including those with type 2 diabetes when portion-controlled.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (1 mg/100 g) and estimated potassium ~230 mg/100 g providing a favourable Na:K ratio consistent with DASH dietary guidelines
hypertension.guidance: Boiled pumpkin is highly suitable for individuals with hypertension due to its negligible sodium content (1 mg/100 g) and potassium-rich profile; avoid adding salt or high-sodium sauces post-cooking to preserve this benefit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High GI (75) but very low glycemic load per serving (GL ~3.7 per 100 g) due to low carbohydrate content (4.9 g/100 g); low energy density (20 kcal/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Boiled pumpkin is suitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes when consumed in standard portions (100–150 g), as the low glycemic load per serving (GL ~3.7–5.6) offsets the moderately high GI; avoid sweetened preparations or added sauces containing sugar.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat (0.04 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, low energy density (20 kcal/100 g), and antioxidant carotenoids including beta-carotene
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Boiled pumpkin is cardioprotective due to its negligible saturated fat, zero cholesterol, low caloric density, and high beta-carotene content; it can be freely included in a heart-healthy dietary pattern without restriction.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Rich in beta-carotene (pro-vitamin A) and antioxidant carotenoids with documented anti-inflammatory properties; negligible saturated fat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Boiled pumpkin is suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its anti-inflammatory carotenoid content and absence of pro-inflammatory saturated fats; it can be consumed freely as part of an anti-inflammatory diet.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Boiled pumpkin is a minimally processed vegetable dish prepared by cooking fresh pumpkin (Cucurbita moschata or Cucurbita maxima) in boiling water, typically with the skin and seeds intact as described in the HPB Singapore database entry. The resulting product has a soft, slightly yielding texture with a mildly sweet, earthy flavour characteristic of cooked cucurbit vegetables. It is a staple side dish in Singaporean home cooking and is widely consumed across Chinese, Malay, and Indian households in Singapore. Nutritionally, boiled pumpkin is very low in calories (20 kcal/100 g), low in fat (0.07 g/100 g), and provides modest carbohydrates (4.9 g/100 g) with a low glycemic load per serving despite a high GI of 75. It is a good source of dietary fibre (1.1 g/100 g), calcium (15 mg/100 g), and iron (0.57 mg/100 g), and is rich in beta-carotene and antioxidant carotenoids. Its negligible sodium content (1 mg/100 g) makes it ideal for sodium-restricted diets. Clinically, it is suitable for individuals managing hypertension, type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis. It is naturally vegetarian, vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher.
common_user_queries: Is boiled pumpkin good for diabetes?
How many calories in boiled pumpkin?
Can I eat boiled pumpkin if I have high blood pressure?
What is the GI of boiled pumpkin?
Nutritional value of boiled pumpkin per 100g
Is boiled pumpkin good for weight loss?
Is pumpkin suitable for a keto diet?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F14070002), which is the primary approved source for Singaporean food records. GI value of 75 is supported by the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature. Confidence is high as the exact food item is present in the primary approved source.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: these fields are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for boiled pumpkin (F14070002) as of 2026. Potassium (230 mg/100 g) and vitamin C (9 mg/100 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central NDB 11423 (Pumpkin, cooked, boiled, drained, without salt) as a secondary cross-reference, as HPB does not report these values; these are marked as estimated. Saturated fat (0.04 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central as HPB does not report fatty acid fractions. Cholesterol is confirmed zero (0.0 mg/100 g) for this plant-based food. Vitamin B12 and vitamin D are confirmed zero (0.0) as this is a plant-based food with no animal-derived components. The dietary tag 'keto' from the input has been retained in dietary_flags context but is borderline given 4.9 g carbohydrate per 100 g; users following strict ketogenic diets should exercise portion control. GI of 75 is high, but glycemic load per 100 g serving is low at 3.7, making this food clinically acceptable for most individuals including those with type 2 diabetes when portion-controlled.
